questions.text: Who is the author of the story 'This is Jody's Fawn'?
questions.explanation: The story is written by Marjorie Kinnan Rawlings.

questions.text: What happened to Jody's father, Penny?
options: He was bitten by a rattlesnake
He was lost in the woods
questions.explanation: Jody's father was bitten by a rattlesnake while in the forest.

questions.text: How did Penny save his own life after the snake bite?
options: By killing a doe and using its heart and liver to draw out the poison
questions.explanation: He used a traditional remedy of using a doe's internal organs to extract the venom.

questions.text: Why was Jody worried about the fawn?
options: He was afraid it would starve to death without its mother
questions.explanation: Since they killed the mother doe, Jody felt responsible for the abandoned fawn's survival.

questions.text: What was the father's reaction when Jody asked to bring the fawn home?
options: He agreed, saying it would be ungrateful to leave it to starve
He said they had no food
questions.explanation: Penny felt a sense of gratitude and guilt, agreeing with Jody's compassionate plea.

questions.text: What did Jody offer to give up so they could keep the fawn?
questions.explanation: Jody promised to share his milk with the fawn as they didn't have much extra food.

questions.text: Who went with Jody on the horse to find the fawn?
questions.explanation: Mill-wheel accompanied Jody into the forest on horseback.

questions.text: Why did Jody want to go alone into the woods at the final stage of the search?
options: He didn't want anyone to see his disappointment or intense joy
He wanted to hide from his father
questions.explanation: Jody wanted the moment of finding (or not finding) the fawn to be private.

questions.text: What did Jody see in the clearing that indicated something had died there?
questions.explanation: A group of buzzards were sitting in a circle around the carcass of the doe.

questions.text: How did the buzzards react when Jody threw a twig at them?
options: They flew into a nearby tree
questions.explanation: They rose in a noisy cloud and settled in the branches of a tree.

questions.text: How did Jody know that the fawn was a male?
options: The spots were all in a line
questions.explanation: Penny had told him that on a buck-fawn, the spots are all in a line.

questions.text: What was the fawn's reaction when Jody first touched it?
questions.explanation: The fawn was startled and shook, but it allowed Jody to touch its neck.

questions.text: How did Jody carry the fawn back through the bushes?
options: He carried it in his arms, shielding its face
He put it in a bag
questions.explanation: He picked it up and tried to protect it from the prickly vines.

questions.text: Why did Jody have to stop and rest while carrying the fawn?
questions.explanation: Jody was a young boy and the physical effort of carrying the fawn was exhausting.

questions.text: What did Jody remember his father saying about fawns?
options: A fawn will follow you if it has first been carried
questions.explanation: This memory encouraged Jody to let the fawn walk some of the distance.

questions.text: When Jody reached home, where did he take the fawn first?
questions.explanation: He wanted to show his father that he had successfully brought the fawn back.

questions.text: What was the fawn's reaction to the stairs?
options: It balked and refused to climb them
questions.explanation: The fawn was scared of the unfamiliar wooden stairs and had to be carried up.

questions.text: How did Jody feed the milk to the fawn?
options: He dipped his fingers in milk and let the fawn suck them
From a bowl on the floor
questions.explanation: The fawn didn't know how to drink from a bowl, so Jody used his fingers to mimic nursing.

questions.text: What did the fawn do with its small hooves while drinking milk?
options: It stamped them impatiently on the floor
questions.explanation: This showed the fawn's eagerness and hunger.

questions.text: Which word best describes Jody's character?
questions.explanation: Jody's concern for the orphaned animal shows his deep empathy.

questions.text: What was the mother's initial reaction when Jody told her about the fawn?
options: She gasped and held her coffee pot in mid-air
questions.explanation: She was shocked by the idea of bringing a wild animal into the house.

questions.text: Who said, 'Nothing in the world ever comes quite free'?
questions.explanation: Doc Wilson said this to support Jody's decision, implying they owed a debt to the doe.

questions.text: What kind of snake bit Penny?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly mentions it was a rattlesnake.

questions.text: What was the 'scrub' mentioned in the story?
options: An area of low trees and bushes
questions.explanation: It refers to the vegetation in the forest where the hunt happened.

questions.text: How did Jody find his way back home?
options: He followed the sound of a bell
questions.explanation: He relied on the physical trail left by the horse.

questions.text: The fawn's skin was ________.
questions.explanation: The author describes the fawn's coat as sleek and clean with a sweet scent of grass.

questions.text: Jody's father's eyes were ________ when Jody spoke to him.
questions.explanation: This indicated that Penny was recovering and feeling better.

questions.text: Why did Jody feel the fawn belonged to him?
options: Because he had found it and saved it
His mother gave it to him
questions.explanation: The shared experience and his effort created a bond of ownership.

questions.text: Mill-wheel offered to give Jody a ride because:
options: He wanted to see the fawn too
Jody was too small to walk that far
questions.explanation: The distance was long and the terrain difficult for a child.

questions.text: The story takes place in a ________ setting.
questions.explanation: The entire story revolves around a homestead near a forest.

questions.text: What did the buzzards do when Jody approached the doe's carcass?
options: They flapped their wings and stayed in the tree
questions.explanation: The large birds hissed as a defensive gesture.

questions.text: How did Jody feel when he finally found the fawn?
questions.explanation: He was overwhelmed with emotion and happiness.

questions.text: What did Jody use to shield the fawn's face from the vines?
questions.explanation: He used his own body to protect the small animal.

questions.text: The fawn's spots were ________.
questions.explanation: Specific to male fawns (bucks), the spots are aligned.

questions.text: Jody's house was located in:
questions.explanation: The family lived in a cabin within a forest clearing.

questions.text: The fawn bleated ________ when Jody walked away for a moment.
questions.explanation: It showed the fawn's dependency and attachment to Jody.

questions.text: What was the name of the author's famous novel from which this might be inspired?
questions.explanation: Marjorie Kinnan Rawlings is best known for 'The Yearling'.

questions.text: The story teaches us about:
options: Moral obligation and kindness toward animals
questions.explanation: The core theme is taking responsibility for the consequences of one's actions.

questions.text: Jody's mother eventually ________ the idea of the fawn.
questions.explanation: Though reluctant, she allowed Jody to go and bring it back.

questions.text: By the end of the story, the fawn is:
options: Safe and being fed at Jody's home
questions.explanation: The story ends with a touching scene of Jody nursing the fawn.
